I heard an interesting thing, though, about how the Shintoism and Buddhism technically a lot of people identify as one or the other, if not both. But people in Japan really are not particularly religious in the way that we would recognize. They don't really have some deep attachment to their religion like people do in North America and other places.

But one explanation I heard is that the true religion of Japan, the unlabeled one, is that if you really wanted to pick the thing that everyone is observing and everyone is taking serious and that guides people's decisions, you could say that the religion of Japan is Japanese society.

And that makes a lot of sense, because even just watching people just a little stuff like watching them on the subway, the politeness and the way people bow to each other and the way people interact. It's like walking on eggshells all the time to make sure you're not overstepping a bound or stepping on someone's toes figuratively.

And almost everyone does it and they do it to a degree that we certainly do not.

And that does feel very much like a religious observance and it is just a sign of how strongly Japan tries to maintain its societal norms.

Didn't need to be scared of the people and you could just go in and possibly nab a chip bag or something. So it was, uh, a bit of an unusual situation. I mean, hopefully I never bump into wild boars ever again.

Because wild animals are scary, man.

So as I approached Camine Park, I came at it from a different angle where I had walked around the back of the power station. And like I said, it just went down to some streets and some houses and stuff and you realize, oh, I'm actually really not in nature at all. But then getting back to Camine Proper, I was like, oh, yeah, I remember now. This is why I thought the falls were like an epic journey when they're only a kilometer and a half away. It's because I did go on an epic journey. I started at a much earlier subway station, walked across a big bridge, went around the tunnel, got lost there a bit, then by the fucking golf course, and then on this wild Indiana Jones super rough pass with these weird cliff sides and all this fucking very dubious shit. And I was like, oh, yeah, that's why. It did take me all day to get there, but yeah, if you don't want to do all that stuff, it's incredibly easy to get there. But it's kind of cool because I just passed by there, which had been, you know, chained off and like, don't go here before. And I didn't really deliberately go on that trail. I just ended up at the other end of it and was like, wait a minute, isn't this that same trail? You know, it was pretty rough. There was a couple scary parts, but it wasn't so bad. And yeah, notice now that the do not enter chain is gone. Now there's a new sign that just says this is not part of Camine Park, but then it has some of the highlights of like, here's the stuff on this trail if you want to go do it. So that's kind of cool. I mean, it wasn't as wildly irresponsible as it seemed at the time. 1:20:00 Now it's just like open again and they're like, yeah, it's a trail man, but just so you know, it's not part of this park. So don't fucking blame us if you'd die. But that's kind of cool. And I guess I'll yammer a little bit about the new Airbnb real quick. So it's just a guy's house, but it's kind of cool because I got like a sneaky, there's like a sneaky back entrance through some hedges. So I can sneak in through there and I got a key to the back door and then I just go straight upstairs. And there's think three rooms, but I think I'm the only one who's in one right now. And then the downstairs is his. So the downsides are, we got to share the shower, but that's not a big deal. It's not like he's in the shower very often. And there is no kitchen really. The kitchen area is just his. But he did set up, I mean partially, maybe this is just to counterbalance the fact that there's no proper kitchen. But part of it is also I think just the that hotelification thing that's been coming up more and more about Airbnb's is there's like a little coffee stand, coffee tea, whatever's got like a little hot water thing like they have at the convenience stores. And it's just like free tea and coffee. Help yourself. And it's like, hey, I mean, that's cool on the one hand, but.

But I'd rather just the things be more bare bones and not have a coffee station, but I would ever do it. I guess it's here, but I thought I'd try one today. And it's weird where the coffee thing is really kind of cool. It's this little pouch. It has like a little hook on it like this little device that you pull out from the bag is kind of like a tea bag. And you hang it on the edge on the inside of your cup and then fill it with water. And it's just like that little little Japanese ingenuity where it's just kind of neat. It's just like, wow, this is a lot more elaborate than any kind of instant coffee type stuff I've ever seen in North America. Any had little creamers, but it's just funny because it's like a little teeny coffee cup. Fairly small little coffee pouch thing. But then all these mega sweet coffees I'm always complaining about, dude, it is not just me. The fucking sugar thing was enormous. This big, big long stick like pouch, like long stick shaped pouch of sugar that is intended for one coffee. It's enough sugar for four coffees easily, but that makes sense. That's how they like their coffee around here. They just wanted to be so fucking sweet. So I didn't even open it. I'm like, I can't even use this. This is a goddamn inhuman amount of sugar. So I just made a coffee with just a little creamer and no sugar at all. And it was okay, but I don't think I'll be going out of my way for the coffee tea fucking little outpost. I'll just go get a coffee as I leave the house, you know.

But yeah, it's kind of weird too just because I don't have any kitchen access besides that. I just wanted to fill a water bottle. So instead, I just refilled the bottle, you know, it was already half full of water. As it still is now, usually when I get home at the end of the day, it's still half full of water.
